data.content_ref: inline:The account attributes the collapse to a synchronized wave of internal rebellion by disenfranchised 'runners' overthrowing chariot-based aristocracies, yet it excludes Egypt on the grounds that Egypt co-opted mercenaries into its military. This exclusion contradicts the claimed mechanism: if the revolutionary energy was driven by population growth, land pressure, and a disenfranchised class, Egypt—with its own dense population, social hierarchy, and documented reliance on Libyan and other mercenary groups—should have been equally or more vulnerable to internal rebellion. The account offers no reason why Egypt's co-option strategy would succeed when the same mercenary groups (e.g., Shardana, Lukka) were supposedly the very agents of destruction elsewhere. If these groups were fundamentally revolutionary, their integration into Egypt's military should have imported the rebellion, not neutralized it.
